key_points: Tribes have unique customs, festivals, and art forms that reflect their culture
Nomadic life involves seasonal migration and dependence on animals for livelihood
Settled communities emerged due to farming and domestication of animals
Daily occupations included hunting, gathering, farming, herding, and tool-making
Trade between communities facilitated cultural exchange and technological progress
Geography influenced settlements, food habits, housing, and lifestyle
examples: The Bhil tribe celebrates the Bhagoria festival with traditional dances
Gujjars migrate across regions with cattle to find grazing lands
Early Ganga valley farmers grew wheat and barley, leading to permanent villages
Tribal art includes wall paintings, pottery, and weaving unique to each region
Barter trade between nomads and settled farmers exchanged grains for milk and dairy products
Himalayan tribes adapted to cold climates by rearing sheep and building sloped houses
formulas: Tribe → Community with shared culture, customs, and traditions
Nomad → Moves seasonally; relies on animals and natural resources
exam_tips: Learn examples of tribal festivals, art, and daily life to illustrate culture
Understand how geography and resources shaped lifestyle and occupations
Know how trade and interaction connected different communities
Focus on the differences and similarities between tribes, nomads, and settled groups
